elements.sent-2411-011: Next-generation mobile networks require evolved radio access network (RAN) architectures to meet the demands of high capacity, massive connectivity, reduced costs, and energy efficiency, and to realize communication with ultra-low latency and ultra-high reliability.
elements.sent-2411-012: One of the potential solutions that attracted significant research attention in the past 15 years is to redesign the radio access network (RAN).
elements.sent-2411-013: In this survey, we present a comprehensive survey on distributed antenna system (DAS) architectures that address these challenges and improve network performance.
elements.sent-2411-014: We cover the transition from traditional decentralized RAN to DAS, including cloud radio-access networks (C-RAN), fog radio-access networks (F-RAN), virtualized radio-access networks (V-RAN), cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-mMIMO), and {the most recent advances manifested in open radio-access network (O-RAN).}
elements.sent-2411-015: In the process, we discuss the benefits and limitations of these architectures, including the impact of limited-capacity fronthaul links, various cooperative uplink and downlink coding strategies, cross-layer optimization, and techniques to optimize the performance of DAS.
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, we review key enabling technologies for next-generation RAN systems, such as multi-access edge computing, network function virtualization, software-defined networking, and network slicing; in addition to some crucial radio access technologies, such as millimeter wave, massive multi-input multi-output, device-to-device communication, and massive machine-type communication.
elements.sent-2411-017: Last but not least, we discuss the major research challenges in DAS and identify several possible directions for future research.
elements.sent-2411-018: This aligns seamlessly with recent statistical projections by Ericsson emphasizing that the anticipated mobile traffic load for the year 2030 and beyond is expected to skyrocket to 5016 exabytes per month, underscoring the surging demand for data-centric services and applications \cite{Eric .}
elements.sent-2411-019: This surge in data consumption is accompanied by the substantial energy consumption footprint of mobile networks, globally estimated at approximately USD 25 billion \cite{Eric2 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: This estimation is further compounded by contemporary global economic challenges, marked by burgeoning inflation and a mounting energy crisis.
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, a comprehensive response, involving both the evolution of communication protocols and technologies as well as a paradigm shift in the design and configuration of existing radio access networks (RANs) is needed.
elements.sent-2411-022: Compared to 5G networks, the transition to 6G networks holds immense promise for enabling unprecedented levels of connectivity, capacity, and intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-023: With data-intensive applications such as augmented reality, virtual reality, IoT, and autonomous systems on the horizon, 6G must enable harnessing the power of artificial intelligence for efficient network management, intelligent resource allocation, and enhanced user experiences, while reducing total operational costs and energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-024: Realizing these requirements in 6G requires supporting stringent Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) including a connection density of $10^7$ users per square kilometer with a 1 Gbps downlink data rate, coupled with network reliability exceeding $99.99999\%$ and communication latency as low as $0.1$ msec \cite{8826541 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Consequently, mobile operators are currently facing increasing pressure to enhance their network performance, prompting them to explore novel solutions in order to keep up with the requirements of 6G.
elements.sent-2411-026: This typically involves either adding new network equipment/modules or adopting more efficient, flexible, and scalable alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-027: Quantitatively, the RAN represents a substantial portion (approximately 65\%-70\%) of total network capital expenditures, according to Ericsson \cite{Eric .}
elements.sent-2411-028: To effectively address the 6G requirements, a revolutionized synergistic approach is required, wherein RAN architectural innovations align harmoniously with robust energy-efficient strategies and sophisticated quality-of-service (QoS) provisions.
elements.sent-2411-029: In light of this transformative imperative for the future, next-generation mobile networks necessitate a meticulous design approach to handle limited radio resources, optimize network parameters, and manage the increasing demands of mobile data traffic.
elements.sent-2411-030: As we move towards 6G, these challenges become more complex, demanding unique and innovative solutions for future network paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-031: These challenges have driven researchers to propose advanced RAN architectures with efficient and powerful processing techniques that can dramatically improve network performance.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.47\textwidth]{6GKPI.png} \caption{{Evolution of mobile generations: A comparative evaluation from 4G KPIs to 6G KPIs.}} \label{6GKPI} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-032: Understanding the RAN architecture could help in determining the most appropriate evolution to keep up with the requirements of 6G and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-033: The landscape of mobile communication has witnessed a remarkable evolution over the years, with each generation bringing forth new demands and technological challenges.
elements.sent-2411-034: One pivotal aspect of this evolution is the continuous transformation of the RAN architecture to meet the diverse KPIs associated with different mobile generations as shown in Fig.~\ref{6GKPI .}
elements.sent-2411-035: In the early 2000s, de-Centralized RAN was deployed as the nascent phase of mobile networks and was synonymous with the 2G and early 3G architectures.
elements.sent-2411-036: In de-Centralized RAN, base stations (BSs) are distributed across the service area, each serving a specific cell.
elements.sent-2411-037: This architecture sufficed for the voice-centric communication predominant during these early stages.
elements.sent-2411-038: The primary KPIs were coverage, capacity, and spectral efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, as mobile networks transitioned to 4G and later 5G, the limitations of de-Centralized RAN became evident.
elements.sent-2411-040: The exponential growth of data-driven applications demanded higher data rates, lower latency, and increased reliability.
elements.sent-2411-041: This triggered the need for a more advanced architecture, leading to the emergence of distributed antenna systems (DASs) \cite{6824752 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: The advent of 4G networks ushered in Centralized RAN, a transformative architecture that revolutionized the way RANs were structured.
elements.sent-2411-043: C-RAN centralized baseband processing in a central unit (CU), while remote radio heads (RRHs) were distributed across the coverage area.
elements.sent-2411-044: This DAS architectural shift enabled more efficient resource management, improved coordination between cells, and greater spectral efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-045: C-RAN was adept at meeting KPIs associated with 4G and early 5G, including higher data rates, lower latency, and improved reliability.
elements.sent-2411-046: The transition to C-RAN also brought about significant advantages in terms of energy efficiency and network optimization \cite{7858136 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, as mobile networks continued to evolve, driven by emerging applications like augmented reality, virtual reality, and autonomous systems, C-RAN struggled to meet the escalating demands for ultra-high data rates, extremely low latency, and robust connectivity in high-density areas.
elements.sent-2411-048: This led to the exploration of alternative architectures, culminating in the emergence of Cell-Free Massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-mMIMO), characterized by the deployment of a multitude of access points (APs) across the service area, effectively eliminating cell boundaries \cite{R5,R7 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Leveraging massive MIMO techniques, this architecture achieved unprecedented spatial multiplexing and interference management.
elements.sent-2411-050: These capabilities made CF-mMIMO well-suited for advanced 5G applications, providing a foundation for immersive experiences and reliable IoT connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-051: The transition from C-RAN to CF-mMIMO underscored the importance of architectural innovation in accommodating evolving mobile generation requirements \cite{R13 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Another viable solution to reduce RAN costs is to exploit network functions virtualization (NFV) and leverage software-based solutions and cloud-native technologies, supported by a virtualized/cloud RAN (V-RAN) architecture to optimize network resources and streamline operations.
elements.sent-2411-053: V-RAN was introduced as a pivotal step toward software-defined networking (SDN) and NFV.
elements.sent-2411-054: This architecture virtualized both baseband processing and radio functions, making network management highly agile and cost-effective \cite{8642354 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: It enabled the deployment of network services as software modules, enhancing adaptability to evolving service requirements.
elements.sent-2411-056: V-RAN excelled in addressing KPIs associated with resource utilization, service agility, and total cost of ownership.
elements.sent-2411-057: The transition from C-RAN to V-RAN marked a critical juncture in the evolution of RAN architectures.
elements.sent-2411-058: It responded to the growing demand for more flexible and scalable networks, capable of rapidly adapting to changing service needs.
elements.sent-2411-059: Virtualization technologies allowed for dynamic resource allocation, efficient scaling of network functions, and the introduction of new services without the need for extensive hardware upgrades \cite{9075181 .}
elements.sent-2411-060: In order to address the demands of low-latency and high-reliability applications, Fog RAN (F-RAN) has gained prominence in recent years as an evolution of traditional RAN designs \cite{7555389,7558153 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Unlike the C-RAN and V-RAN, F-RAN takes a partially decentralized approach, pushing a significant portion of network processing functions closer to the edge of the network.
elements.sent-2411-062: In F-RAN, computing resources and network functions are strategically distributed across the network, typically in proximity to BSs or APs, where processing occurs closer to where data is generated or consumed.
elements.sent-2411-063: This architecture facilitates lower-latency and high-reliability processing of data and applications, rendering it highly suitable for emerging use cases like ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC).
elements.sent-2411-064: Applications benefiting from this approach include autonomous vehicles, industrial automation, and augmented reality, especially in the context of 5G and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-065: This approach also optimizes resource utilization, reduces network congestion, and enhances the overall QoS.
elements.sent-2411-066: F-RAN is also aligned with the growing trend of multi-access edge computing, which leverages edge resources for various services \cite{7537176, 9861780, 9824969 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: This approach aligns with the evolving requirements of modern mobile generations, making F-RAN a significant player in the future of mobile communication.
elements.sent-2411-068: As the landscape of mobile communication continues to evolve, the need for openness, interoperability, and innovation in RAN architecture becomes increasingly pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-069: Enter Open RAN (O-RAN), a paradigm-shifting approach that embraces the principles of virtualization, standardization, and openness to unleash the full potential of mobile networks.
elements.sent-2411-070: O-RAN redefines the traditional vendor-specific, monolithic RAN equipment by disaggregating network components into interoperable, open interfaces \cite{10024837 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: It empowers network operators to mix and match components from various vendors, fostering competition and driving innovation.
elements.sent-2411-072: This approach enhances network flexibility, scalability, and cost-effectiveness while accommodating the diverse KPIs of modern mobile communication.
elements.sent-2411-073: One of the primary drivers for the adoption of O-RAN is its ability to meet the requirements of advanced 5G and upcoming 6G networks.
elements.sent-2411-074: With the proliferation of diverse applications, from URLLC for mission-critical services to massive machine-type communication (mMTC) for IoT, O-RAN provides the agility to tailor network resources according to specific needs.
elements.sent-2411-075: Furthermore, O-RAN addresses the challenges posed by the growing complexity of RAN management and the need for rapid deployment of new services.
elements.sent-2411-076: By fostering an open ecosystem of vendors and developers, it encourages the creation of innovative solutions that can be seamlessly integrated into the network \cite{10071958 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: It is crystal clear that the evolution of RAN architectures is crucial to ensure that mobile networks meet the evolving demands of different mobile generations.
elements.sent-2411-078: Each architecture represents a step forward in addressing specific challenges and optimizing performance, ultimately shaping the future of wireless communication.
elements.sent-2411-079: Researchers and network operators must continue to assess and innovate in this field to meet the diverse needs of modern mobile communication.
elements.sent-2411-080: As we delve into this survey in the subsequent sections, we will explore promising technologies and techniques contributing to the realization of advanced DAS architectures.
elements.sent-2411-081: This includes the implementation of flexible functional splits between a central processing unit (CPU) and a radio unit (RU), utilization of advanced coding strategies for both uplink and downlink transmissions, cross-layer optimization techniques, and the application of sophisticated resource allocation algorithms and hybrid architectures.
elements.sent-2411-082: Through these advancements, the groundwork is laid for fulfilling the needs of next-generation mobile networks.
elements.sent-2411-083: In this section, we provide a clear roadmap of the survey's focus, encompassing key aspects such as survey scope and performance metrics employed to evaluate performance, a thorough review of related works, the primary contributions offered by our survey, and an overview of the survey's organizational structure.
elements.sent-2411-084: The aim of this survey is to serve as a reference point for readers interested in DAS to help them in navigating the state-of-the-art in this area.
elements.sent-2411-085: This survey discusses the capabilities of DAS architectures, including C-RANs, CF-MIMO, V-RANs, F-RANs, and O-RANs.
elements.sent-2411-086: Additionally, it discusses how DAS benefits from {complemetary technologies such as virtualization, cloud-native networking, device-to-device (D2D) communications, reconfigurable intelligent surfaces {(RISs) , and non-terrestrial networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Moreover, it presents future research directions and challenges associated with the deployment of DAS architectures, {highlighting potential developments in DAS systems and architectures.}

elements.sent-2411-102: Since distributing the antenna terminals in the service area contributes significantly to improving the network's energy efficiency, achievable rates, system coverage, and fairness, we discuss how DASs play a significant role in improving them as follows.
elements.sent-2411-103: Energy Efficiency: In general, energy efficiency is considered a crucial metric that balances the need for high-performance data transmission and the low power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-104: It is essential for the sustainability of wireless networks, especially with the growing demand for mobile data and the expansion of the IoTs.
elements.sent-2411-105: It is common in the literature that the energy efficiency metric is formulated as the total data transmitted over the entire network relative to the total energy consumed by the network.
elements.sent-2411-106: Considering only the transmission power, massive MIMO systems are considered energy-efficient due to the high array gain that improves the system throughput without consuming more transmit power \cite{R21 .}
elements.sent-2411-107: This property can be attained in massive MIMO systems whether the antennas are co-located or distributed \cite{R20 .}
elements.sent-2411-108: However, in the distributed case (DAS), the power consumed in fronthaul links in DASs reduces the energy efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-109: On the other hand, bringing the antennas closer to the users in DASs reduces the transmit power required to achieve the required QoS.
elements.sent-2411-110: Therefore, the main factor that affects the energy efficiency in DASs is the power consumption in the fronthaul links, which becomes a main concern if the number of antennas is large.
elements.sent-2411-111: In other words, distributing the antennas close to the users improves the energy efficiency of the radio access link, but degrades the overall energy efficiency due to the fronthaul links' power consumption\cite{R32, R33 .}
elements.sent-2411-112: System Throughput: The achievable rates in DASs are significantly improved due to several factors.
elements.sent-2411-113: First, since the antennas are physically distributed and connected to a CPU through \text{fronthaul links, the \text{CPU can process the received/transmitted signals \text{of the antennas jointly to completely eliminate or mitigate the interference, which leads to a significant increase in the achievable rates at the users.}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Second, the channels between the antennas and the users are more likely to be of high quality due to the small distances between them.
elements.sent-2411-115: In addition, increasing the number of distributed antennas improves the macro-diversity that helps in reducing the outage probability \cite{8972478, R3, R45 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: Coverage Probability: Distributing the antennas over a wide area also guarantees to improve the coverage \text{and reduces blind spots that are caused by blockages .}
elements.sent-2411-117: This improves the coverage probability significantly since if a user has no line-of-sight to some APs, it would have line-of-sight to others \cite{8972478 .}
elements.sent-2411-118: System Fairness: DASs also improve system fairness by generating a uniform deliverable QoS for all the users in the considered area.
elements.sent-2411-119: In particular, the cooperation between APs removes the cell boundaries and hence avoids having users at the edge {of a cell where they would suffer high interference and weak channel conditions \cite{R6 .}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Maximizing the sum rate in DAS requires careful optimization {of user scheduling and power allocation, which was shown to be a challenging task \cite{CLNC1, 4068001, 4769397, 5464889 .}}
elements.sent-2411-121: To offload the cloud's tasks to different nodes in the DAS, popular files can be cached on the network edge (i.e., close to the end users) such as at enhanced RUs or at the users.
elements.sent-2411-122: The file delivery delay metric is {most important in applications that require immediate delivery of data for real-time applications, {such as live video streaming.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: In the literature, this delay metric is defined, based on the application, as the number of transmissions \cite{7845689, 8794557, 7511238, 9512273, 9180294, 8267199, 8444481, 6882208, 1176612, 4557282, 1705002, 4567144, 4675714 or the latency in seconds \cite{RAIDNC1, 4567144, RAIDNC2, 6662474, RAIDNC3, RAIDNC4, 6692154, RAIDNC5 .}}
elements.sent-2411-124: This enhances the efficiency and responsiveness of data delivery, especially in time-sensitive applications, by devising strategies and algorithms that minimize file delivery delay.

elements.sent-2411-147: We summarize our contributions in this survey paper as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-148: We comprehensively review the evolution of DAS architectures from de-centralized RAN to C-RAN, V-RAN, F-RAN, and CF-MIMO to O-RAN.
elements.sent-2411-149: We also present the motivation for reconstructing and redesigning legacy DAS architectures, the state-of-the-art, the ongoing working groups, and standardization activities to satisfy the stringent requirements of next-generation RAN.
elements.sent-2411-150: We provide a literature review of the design of the fronthaul network demonstrating the limitations of conventional fronthaul interfaces to satisfy the staggeringly soaring capacity and latency demands.
elements.sent-2411-151: Additionally, we explore potential solutions to tackle this issue from various perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-152: We start by investigating the main four functional split standards that cover the evolution of DAS networks, including 3GPP \cite{3GPP1 , eCPRI \cite{eCPRI1 , SCF \cite{SCF , and the O-RAN Alliance \cite{ORAN-FH .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: We {then highlight how flexible functional split can satisfy the continuously changing network requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Moreover, we provide comparative analysis for various uplink and downlink coding strategies in DAS.
elements.sent-2411-155: We review works that focused on optimizing DAS networks via optimizing beamforming matrices the channel estimation operations to improve their performance in terms of achievable rates, energy efficiency, and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-156: In addition, we explore recent works that integrated the DAS concept into emerging 6G networks such as non-terrestrial and RIS-assisted wireless networks.
elements.sent-2411-157: In addition to the physical layer perspective, we discuss DAS architectures from a cross-layer perspective through cross-layer NC (CLNC) that exploits NC decisions, rate adaptation, and power allocation.
elements.sent-2411-158: Last but not least, we {overview existing challenges and future research directions related to different DAS architectures, {with the aim of motivating the academic and industry community towards realistic solutions with new technological advances.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: This paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-160: In Section~\ref{Design and Section~\ref{Split , we review the design requirements of the fronthaul network in DAS by covering the conventional fronthaul interfaces and functional split standards.}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Section~\ref{Uplink discusses uplink and downlink coding strategies used in DAS to relay signals between users and the CPU via the distributed RUs.}
elements.sent-2411-162: System-level optimization for different DAS architectures is discussed in Section~\ref{Tec with focus on channel estimation, transmit precoding and receive combining techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-163: The integration of DAS with other advanced communication technologies is discussed in Section~\ref{Integ .}
elements.sent-2411-164: Cross-layer optimization for resource allocation in different DAS architectures is surveyed in Section~\ref{Cross with focus on graph theory techniques and CLNC.}
elements.sent-2411-165: An outlook on future research directions is provided in Section~\ref{Fut , before closing this paper with conclusions in Section~\ref{C .}}
elements.sent-2411-166: The outline of this survey paper is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{Outline .}

elements.section-2411-003: Design of Fronthaul Network in Distributed Antenna Systems
elements.sent-2411-168: Because of the centralized nature of the processing resources in the CPU and the distributed nature of the antenna terminals in the service area, fronthaul links between the distributed RUs and the CPU are of critical importance in the design of DAS architectures.
elements.sent-2411-169: In order to build a high-reliability fronthaul network, it is necessary to take into account some interdependent technical requirements such as the RU configuration, required data rate, bit error rate (BER), latency, synchronization, and jitter, and operations administration and maintenance constraints.
elements.sent-2411-170: It is also important to consider business aspects in terms of obtaining low-cost implementation from a mobile operator point of view \cite{7009970 .}
elements.sent-2411-171: In this section, we start by presenting traditional fronthaul interface standards and highlighting their constraints, followed by how we can overcome their limitations to keep up with the requirements of next-generation RAN as discussed next.
elements.sent-2411-172: The conventional fronthaul interface has been standardized by the CPRI and OBSAI specifications \cite{7096298 .}
elements.sent-2411-173: Both protocols include different RAN vendor-specific groups, thereby, full interoperability between them is not guaranteed \cite{7331128 .}
elements.sent-2411-174: The first version of the CPRI protocol was published in 2003 as a result of cooperation between leading RAN technologies vendors, e.g. Ericsson, Huawei Technologies Co.
elements.sent-2411-176: Further improved versions have been released until version 7.0 in 2015 \cite{CPRI1 .}
elements.sent-2411-177: CPRI specifications divide the BS into two parts including a baseband unit (BBU), or a radio equipment control (REC) unit (can be referred to as CPU), and a radio frequency unit or radio access equipment (RE).
elements.sent-2411-178: The CPRI interface separates between the radio-frequency (RF) signal and baseband signal using the physical layer (L1) and data-link layer (L2) protocols of the open standard interconnection (OSI) protocol stack \cite{7858136,9489948 .}
elements.sent-2411-179: However, CPRI has a stringent BER constraint, where each fronthaul link must operate with at most a BER value of $10^{-12}$ \cite{7009970,7402275 .}
elements.sent-2411-180: In contrast, the OBSAI is another industry specification group resulting from the collaboration of joining RAN vendors, and component and module manufacturers to reduce the cost of deploying RAN architectures \cite{tt .}
elements.sent-2411-181: OBSAI was first released in 2002 and successive improved versions have been published afterwards as for CPRI.
elements.sent-2411-182: According to the OBSAI specifications, the BS is divided into four main modules including RF, transmission, processing, and control modules.
elements.sent-2411-183: The RF module transmits, receives, and amplifies RF signals and converts them from analog to digital and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-184: The transmission module provides communicating interfaces between the different modules, as well as the conformance and interoperability test specifications.
elements.sent-2411-185: In the processing module, the baseband signals are computed and processed.
elements.sent-2411-186: The control module supports system management, configuration, and synchronization of all BS modules and generates reports to the operations administration and maintenance system \cite{8527624 .}
elements.sent-2411-187: Therefore, OBSAI specifications deal with L1, L2, transport, and application layers protocols of the OSI protocol stack \cite{inbook1 .}
elements.sent-2411-188: Also, the BER requirement in OBSAI is $10^{-15}$ which is more stringent than that in CPRI.
elements.sent-2411-189: Another industry specification group called ORI was formed by the European Telecommunications Standards Institute in 2010 to introduce a fronthaul interface specification that can support multi-vendor interoperability between network elements in the RAN system \cite{ORI,7331128 .}
elements.sent-2411-190: This interface is built over the CPRI protocol by removing some functions and adding other options to support the full-interoperability \cite{7009970 .}
elements.sent-2411-191: Despite the differences between the CPRI, OBSAI, and ORI interfaces, they all provide a constant bit rate in both uplink and downlink transmission.
elements.sent-2411-192: However, CPRI is the widely used protocol for the fronthaul interface by RAN vendors, especially for conventional fully-centralized 4G C-RANs, due to its efficient one-to-one in-phase/quadrature-phase samples mapping techniques between the distributed RU and the CPU \cite{7858136,7414147 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: As a result, in this subsection, we confine our investigation to the CPRI specifications as a case study of the conventional fronthaul interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-194: In conventional 4G C-RAN networks, the CPRI fronthaul interface is used to fully separate the RaF functions in distributed RU and all the baseband processing functions in the CPU as shown in Fig. \ref{Fig:CPRI \cite{7402275 .}}
elements.sent-2411-195: This split allows the full centralization of all L1/L2/L3 processing functions of the OSI protocol stack, at the expense of extremely stringent fronthaul capacity and latency demands \cite{ITU .}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{CPRIsplit1.png} \caption{CPRI RAN functional split: fully separating RaF functions in distributed RU (RE) and all L1/L2/L3 processing functions of the OSI protocol stack in the CPU (REC).} \label{Fig:CPRI}
elements.sent-2411-196: CPRI can support constant data rate options from 614.4 Mbps up to 24330.24 Mbps over one RU-CPU fronthaul connection \cite{7009970,Pfeiffer:15 .}
elements.sent-2411-197: Generally, information flows at the higher layers of the OSI protocol stack are less data-intensive than those at the lowest.
elements.sent-2411-198: Processing functions in the physical (PHY) layer (e.g. cyclic redundancy check, modulation, mapping, and encoding) add extra overhead to the data blocks received from the higher media access control (MAC) layer.
elements.sent-2411-199: Thus, incredibly higher data rates are required as the information flows at the PHY layer towards the RU where RaF functions are performed \cite{CPRI1 .}
elements.sent-2411-200: Practically, CPRI requires an overhead of up to 30 times the 4G LTE baseband data rates, thus requiring the deployment of one or more 10 Gbps optical fiber fronthaul links to support 4G LTE data rates \cite{Chanclou2013OpticalFS .}
elements.sent-2411-201: As current 5G networks aim to support 10 times the data rates of LTE, the fronthaul rate needs an increase to over 100 Gbps, which is cost-prohibitive and operationally challenging.
elements.sent-2411-202: Particularly, the required CPRI data rate $R_{CPRI}$ depends on the employed radio-access technology, MIMO implementation, and RF channel bandwidth as shown in the following expression \cite{7009970,Oliveira2014AnalysisOT \begin{equation}R_{CPRI} = 2 S_n A f_s b_s C_w C,\end{equation} where $S_n$ is the number of sectors, $A$ represents the number of antenna ports per sector, $f_s$ is the sampling rate for RF signal digitization (30.72 Mega samples/second per 20 MHz RF channel bandwidth) and $b_s$ is the number of bits per sample (referred as the sample width and is typically 15 bits/sample for LTE systems), $C_w$ accounts for control word overhead in the CPRI frames (typically 16/15), and $C$ denotes a line coding factor (either 10/8 for 8B/10B code or 66/64 for 64B/66B code) \cite{3GPP1,7009970 .}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}R_{CPRI} = 2 S_n A f_s b_s C_w C,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-203: The factor 2 is due to the in-phase and quadrature phase components.
elements.sent-2411-204: For instance, for high bandwidth demanding services such as enhanced mobile broadband (eMBB) or for massive MIMO distributed RUs, the CPRI protocol requires unreasonably high fronthaul capacity with maximum latency between RU and CPU of a few hundred microseconds.
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Required CPRI fronthaul data rate in fully centralized RAN \cite{3GPP1}} \begin{tabular}{P{0.2\textwidth}P{0.1\textwidth}P{0.1\textwidth}P{0.1\textwidth}P{0.1\textwidth}P{0.1\textwidth}P{0.1\textwidth}} \multirow{2}{*}{Number of antennas} \multicolumn{6}{c}{RF channel bandwidth} 10 MHz 20 MHz 50 MHz 100 MHz 200 MHz 1 GHz 2 1 Gbps 2 Gbps 5 Gbps 100 Gbps 20 Gbps 100 Gbps 8 4 Gbps 8 Gbps 20 Gbps 40 Gbps 80 Gbps 400 Gbps 64 32 Gbps 64 Gbps 160 Gbps 320 Gbps 640 Gbps 3200 Gbps 256 128 Gbps 256 Gbps 640 Gbps 1280 Gbps 2560 Gbps 12800 Gbps \label{table:CPRI1} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-205: Table~\ref{table:CPRI1 demonstrates the approximate required CPRI data rates without line coding using various RF channel bandwidths and numbers of antennas in a conventional fully-centralized C-RAN as given by the 3GPP.}
elements.sent-2411-206: As shown in this table, with 20 MHz LTE and 2 antennas configuration, the required CPRI data rate without line coding is 2 Gbps (2.5 Gbps with line coding 10/8) for one RU-CPU fronthaul connection.
elements.sent-2411-207: Therefore, extremely high fronthaul capacity is needed in order to have a fully centralized C-RAN which is a major problem in the design of next-generation RANs \cite{LTE1,6897914 .}
elements.sent-2411-208: Moreover, the 4G LTE RAN latency requirement of about 10 ms leaves a delay budget of 100 microseconds over the fronthaul link between the CPU and RU; conversely, the maximum fronthaul distance is only around 15 km.
elements.sent-2411-209: With some 5G applications, an RAN latency of 1 ms is required.
elements.sent-2411-210: This requirement forces the distance between the RU and the CPU to be around 1 km, requiring many more locations for the CPUs, which is expensive \cite{8367785 .}
elements.sent-2411-211: CPRI specifications have some other drawbacks that make them too far from accommodating the evolution in the RAN industry nowadays.
elements.sent-2411-212: First, CPRI has a constant data rate regardless of the change of mobile traffic per the temporal/service area dimensions \cite{CPRI1 .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Even in situations when there is no active user traffic in the network, there are still CPRI streams forwarded between the CPU and distributed RUs.
elements.sent-2411-214: This underutilizes the fronthaul bandwidth thus decreasing efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-215: Second, as the number of antennas increases, the required CPRI data rate is incredibly increasing.
elements.sent-2411-216: This is a major impediment to the use of the CPRI interface in 5G networks as far as deploying CF-mMIMO networks is concerned.
elements.sent-2411-217: Moreover, advanced networking and operations administration and maintenance features of data transport standards are required which are not supported by CPRI specifications \cite{eCPRI1 .}
elements.sent-2411-218: Finally, the cost of the fully-centralized DAS networks supported by the CPRI specifications is very high \cite{7414147 .}
elements.sent-2411-219: With the current revolution in 5G/6G networks, many mobile operators started to question the efficiency of CPRI specifications to keep up with the requirements of next-generation RAN.
elements.sent-2411-220: Currently, researchers and industry experts are working on proposing new techniques for lowering the extremely high capacity demands on the fronthaul network, while still keeping as many benefits as possible from the fully centralized processing as in conventional C-RAN.
elements.sent-2411-221: To efficiently utilize the fronthaul network resources and better support the evolving needs of next-generation RAN, advanced functional split schemes, uplink and downlink relaying techniques, hybrid DAS and other emerging technologies, and machine learning and artificial intelligence approaches have been investigated as discussed in the following sections.
elements.section-2411-004: Functional Split Decomposition in DAS networks
elements.sent-2411-222: In next-generation RAN, the mobile traffic is rapidly growing to volumes that exceed the capabilities of conventional fronthaul interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-223: To overcome this constraint, one solution is to include more processing functions at the distributed RUs to process their observed signals more before forwarding them to the CPU via the fronthaul network.
elements.sent-2411-224: However, the critical questions here are, how many processing functions should be included locally at RUs? and to which extent may this help in relaxing the extremely high capacity demands on the fronthaul links? To find proper answers, we must investigate all possible functional splits which determine the amount of PHY/MAC processing functions included locally at the RU and the amount of the centralized functions at the CPU.
elements.sent-2411-225: It is thus critical that the functional split between the RU and CPU should take into account some cost-effective and technical trade-offs between the required fronthaul capacity, latency, and signal processing centralization \cite{8384342 .}
elements.sent-2411-226: In fact, the choice of how to split the RF and baseband processing functions in the DAS architecture depends on the QoS of the supported services (e.g. low/high throughput, low/high latency, and real/non-real-time applications) and the available transport network infrastructure (e.g. optical fiber/wireless networks including microwave, millimeter waves, or free-space optics).
elements.sent-2411-227: Moreover, it must support specific load demand or user density per given geographical service area.
elements.sent-2411-228: Fig.~\ref{Fig:3GFS illustrates the RF and baseband signal processing functions in the LTE protocol stack (still valid for the 5G new radio) and different functional split options provided by 3GPP \cite{3GPP1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-229: In this figure, the signal processing chain closer to the RU is located on the right, and moving to the left means that the observed signal at the RU goes through more and more local processing before forwarding it to the CPU via the fronthaul network.
elements.sent-2411-230: The dashed arrows within Fig.~\ref{Fig:3GFS indicate the different function split options, where the processing functions on the right of the red arrow are the functions performed locally in the RU, and the remaining functions on the left of the dashed arrow are centrally implemented in the CPU.}
elements.sent-2411-231: The more processing functions left locally in the RU, the more signal processing is done before forwarding signals on the fronthaul network to the CPU, thereby lowering the data-rate and latency demands over the fronthaul link \cite{8479363 .}
elements.sent-2411-232: At the same time, this will increase the complexity of the RU configuration, limiting us from deploying cheaper, simpler, and low-power nodes.
elements.sent-2411-233: Here is where the optimization of the tradeoffs becomes paramount.
elements.sent-2411-234: Understanding the protocols included in the CPU could help in determining the appropriate functional split.
elements.sent-2411-235: The conventional CPU support all five protocol layers of the RAN.
elements.sent-2411-236: The physical layer or PHY is mainly responsible for coding and modulation operations for the radio transmission.
elements.sent-2411-237: The actual radio transmission is performed by the RU.
elements.sent-2411-238: For every transmit interval, the MAC layer defines the size to be used.
elements.sent-2411-239: This transmit interval is referred to as transmit block.
elements.sent-2411-240: The MAC is responsible for the reliable transfer of data over the air.
elements.sent-2411-241: The MAC layer performs a hybrid automatic repeat request (HARQ) procedure, which uses acknowledgments and negative acknowledgments feedback from the receiver.
elements.sent-2411-242: HARQ allows the transmitter to retransmit the data packet if the packet is not correctly received.
elements.sent-2411-243: The MAC layer is also used for implementing fast control signaling, for example, activating carrier aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-244: Fast control activation is required because other signaling mechanisms through the upper radio resource controller (RRC) can be almost 10 times slower.
elements.sent-2411-245: The radio link controller (RLC) layer processes the data arriving from the upper layers into packets that fit into the transmit block defined by the MAC layer.
elements.sent-2411-246: An incoming packet may be segmented into smaller packets or multiple packets may be concatenated into a larger packet.
elements.sent-2411-247: The packet data convergence protocol (PDCP) provides optional internet protocol packet header compression and security features.
elements.sent-2411-248: It encrypts the data that is sent over the air and protects the integrity of signaling messages to protect against malicious tempering.
elements.sent-2411-249: RRC is the top protocol layer used for major RAN functions, such as connection establishment, handover procedures, paging idle user equipment (UE), uplink power control, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-250: The PHY, MAC, and RLC layers are involved in tasks that are very delay-sensitive (of the order of a few milliseconds), such as HARQ operations and fast control signaling.
elements.sent-2411-251: In contrast, the higher layer PDCP and RRC protocols undertake tasks that are more delay tolerant and can take tens of milliseconds.
elements.sent-2411-252: Several functional split standards have been proposed for next-generation RANs (e.g. dense small cells, CF-mMIMO, and O-RAN) that allow for a radical reduction of the required fronthaul bandwidth compared to the conventional fully-centralized approach \cite{8384342 .}
elements.sent-2411-253: In this section, we investigate the functional split options proposed by four groups: 3GPP, eCPRI, the SCF, and xRAN (which became the O-RAN Alliance in June 2018).
elements.sent-2411-254: We consider the 3GPP signal processing model \cite{3GPP1 and its functional split options introduced therein as a reference architecture for our investigation of all functional split standards.}
elements.sent-2411-255: In Fig.~\ref{Fig:FS , we map the functional split options in the 3GPP model \cite{3GPP1 to the functional split points introduced in the eCPRI specification by the CPRI group \cite{eCPRI1 , SCF standard \cite{SCF , and the xRAN (O-RAN) group \cite{ORAN-FH .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: Fig.~\ref{Fig:FS shows that the intra-PHY functional split point is further split into multiple sub-options at all standards.}
elements.sent-2411-257: A more detailed mapping and comparison between the intra-PHY functional splits for different functional split standards is shown in Fig.~\ref{Fig:subFS .}
elements.sent-2411-258: This is to support more cooperation between all distributed RUs in the service area and provide more flexibility to meet the fronthaul network requirements as discussed next.

elements.sent-2411-259: In 4G/LTE networks, the RF and baseband processing functions included in the 3GPP protocol stack are divided into a distributed unit (DU) and a CU \cite{3GPP1 .}
elements.sent-2411-260: The CU includes the BBU functions specified by LTE standard (still valid for 5G New Radio) like transmission of user data, mobility management, resource sharing and allocation, session control, etc., except those functions allocated exclusively to the DU.
elements.sent-2411-261: The CU also manages the operation of DUs over the fronthaul transport network.
elements.sent-2411-262: The CU may also be referred to as BBU, CPU, or REC.
elements.sent-2411-263: In contrast, the DU includes part of the baseband processing functions besides the RF functions, depending on the proposed functional split option.
elements.sent-2411-264: This split allows for the coordination of performance features such as required throughput, latency, and cost.
elements.sent-2411-265: In 3GPP, DU may be referred to as RRH, RE, RU, or AP.

elements.section-2411-005: Uplink and Downlink Coding Strategies in DAS
elements.sent-2411-587: One way to limit the fronthaul signaling requirements is through using efficient uplink and downlink source and channel coding schemes.
elements.sent-2411-588: From an information-theoretic perspective, the DAS model is best understood as a relay network.
elements.sent-2411-589: The RUs can be considered relaying nodes that facilitate communication between the CPU and the user terminals.
elements.sent-2411-590: A major drawback of DAS architectures is that their performance is constrained by the limited capacity of fronthaul links that connect the distributed RUs with the CPU.
elements.sent-2411-591: This puts a constraint on the amount of exchanged traffic between the RUs and the CPU.
elements.sent-2411-592: In order to tackle this problem, various uplink relaying schemes were proposed to relay user data traffic from distributed RUs to the CPU, such as DF \cite{quek_peng_simeone_yu_2017 , AF, CF \cite{6342931 , CoF \cite{6522158 , and noisy network coding \cite{5752460 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-593: Additionally, in the downlink, the CPU transmits data to users via the distributed RUs using cooperative relaying schemes such as data-sharing techniques \cite{6920005 , compression-based strategies, \cite{6588350 , RCoF \cite{6283033 , and RQCoF \cite{6522158 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-594: These techniques have gained significant attention in recent years due to their potential to enhance the overall network performance.
elements.sent-2411-595: However, despite the ongoing effort, the optimal relaying strategy is still to be found \cite{8432465 .}
elements.sent-2411-596: The interesting but essential questions here are: What is the appropriate way for the RUs to relay as much useful information as possible to the CPU while satisfying the limited fronthaul capacity constraints of the fronthaul links? Is it using local detection, compression, or computation? How can the physical and data link layer design of a DAS architecture adapt to the capacity limitations of the fronthaul links? Also, what should the CPU do to recover the original messages? Should it use successive or joint decoding? To properly answer these questions, in this section, we discuss various uplink and downlink relaying strategies that utilize the limited fronthaul capacity constraints in different ways for different DAS architectures, along with their corresponding optimization methods and frameworks for finding appropriate solutions.
elements.sent-2411-597: We conclude by providing design insights learned from such optimization.
elements.sent-2411-598: In the uplink, multiple users communicate their codewords with the CPU through the distributed RUs.
elements.sent-2411-599: In an ideal case with infinite-capacity fronthaul links, each RU can forward its exact observed signal to the CPU, and the full joint-decoding gain can be achieved.
elements.sent-2411-600: Therefore, the resulting uplink channel is modeled as an instance of a single-input-multiple-output (SIMO) or MIMO multiple-access channel.
elements.sent-2411-601: In practice, however, the fronthaul links have limited capacity constraints, thus each RU should relay as much useful information as possible from its observation to the CPU in order to mitigate the inter-cell interference and improve the overall network performance.
elements.sent-2411-602: As a result, from an information-theoretic point of view, the uplink channel is modeled in this case as a two-hop multiple-access relay channel \cite{8114545 .}
elements.sent-2411-603: Different relaying strategies can be used to relay traffic from RUs to the CPU.
elements.sent-2411-604: The first typical approach is to make each RU decodes the codewords of its scheduled users, and then re-encodes and forwards them for collaborative transmission to the CPU; this is the so-called DF strategy \cite{1499041 .}
elements.sent-2411-605: Although this technique offers significant advantages, the RU is ultimately interference-limited as the number of transmitted codewords increases.
elements.sent-2411-606: This also scarifies the potential benefits that the user terminals can gain from the jointly centralized processing capability at the CPU \cite{quek_peng_simeone_yu_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-607: In addition, it introduces additional delay and requires high signal processing capability at the distributed RUs which significantly increases the processing power as well as the network complexity and cost, which can be a disadvantage in systems with limited computational resources \cite{7573000 .}
elements.sent-2411-608: Also, DF is sensitive to errors in the received signal.
elements.sent-2411-609: If the RU node is unable to correctly decode the signal, it will not be able to forward it to the CPU, which can result in transmission errors and loss of data \cite{4305394 .}
elements.sent-2411-610: Higher data rates can be attained by decoding integer linear combinations of simultaneously transmitted users' codewords that ``mimic'' the channel realization at each RU, as long as they are all drawn from the same linear codebook; this is the so-called CoF strategy.

elements.sent-2411-612: The key idea of the CoF scheme is that user terminals encode their messages using nested lattice linear codes, and an RU suppresses interference by not decoding the users' codewords but rather computes a weighted sum function (integer linear combination) of them and forwards it to the CPU \cite{6034734 .}

elements.sent-2411-616: However, this strategy is sensitive to channel estimation errors and requires the integer coefficients matrix to be invertible \cite{5205840 .}
elements.sent-2411-617: In the original CF \cite{6034734 , all constructed nested lattice codes at user terminals share a common coarse lattice, thereby all users are constrained by the same power constraint.}

elements.sent-2411-675: In de-centralized RAN, each scheduled user is served by a single BS and experiences interference from all neighboring BSs.
elements.sent-2411-676: The benefit of centralized signal processing in DAS architectures arises from the ability of multiple RUs to cooperatively serve users, which minimizes the effect of unwanted interference.
elements.sent-2411-677: Since the messages intended for different users in DAS, all originate from the CPU, the CPU can relay useful information about the user messages to the distributed RUs via the fronthaul links, thus allowing the RUs to perform network-wide beamforming in order to achieve interference mitigation and cooperative transmission.
