elements.sent-2411-689: A significant improvement can be obtained using common messages and rate-splitting optimization techniques \cite{5997324,8359129,8732995 .}
elements.sent-2411-690: Alternatively, since all the users' data are available at the CPU, the encoded and beamformed signals can be centrally constructed at the CPU.
elements.sent-2411-691: Subsequently, these signals can be compressed and transmitted to the RUs for further processing \cite{6588350 .}
elements.sent-2411-692: Particularly, the signals beamformed at the CPU have the potential to emulate the benefits of full cooperation.
elements.sent-2411-693: However, the beamformed signals become analog instead of discrete like the raw data in the data-sharing strategy.
elements.sent-2411-694: Therefore, it is necessary to compress these signals before transmitting them over digital fronthaul links with limited capacity.
elements.sent-2411-695: The primary benefit of the compression-based approach is the efficient utilization of fronthaul capacity when transmitting beamformed signals containing multiple users' messages over the fronthaul link.
elements.sent-2411-696: However, the downside of this approach is the introduced compression distortion, which leads to a decrease in the achievable downlink rates.
elements.sent-2411-697: In general, the reduction in the achievable rates is influenced by the compression rate, which is determined by the capacity of the fronthaul links and the compression strategy implemented at the CPU \cite{quek_peng_simeone_yu_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-698: Further improvement can be achieved through the implementation of the multivariate compression, where the baseband signals are jointly compressed at the CPU \cite{6588350 .}
elements.sent-2411-699: This approach enables correlation among the compression distortions in the signals of different RUs.
elements.sent-2411-700: By designing the correlation to cancel out compression distortions at the user side, the achievable rates for users can be improved as the added distortions {(due to interference) are mitigated.}
elements.sent-2411-701: In the context of downlink transmission in CF-mMIMO networks, compression strategies remain favorable.
elements.sent-2411-702: Two compression approaches have been explored in CF-mMIMO.
elements.sent-2411-703: The first approach is CAP \cite{8690797,8902784,8678745 , where the centralized signal is first precoded and then compressed at the CPU before being transmitted to the APs.}
elements.sent-2411-704: This makes CAP suitable for centralized precoding.
elements.sent-2411-705: The second approach is precoding-after-compress \cite{10.1007/s11277-018-6038-1 , where simple compression is performed at the CPU by separately quantizing the symbols for each user.}
elements.sent-2411-706: Each AP then receives the symbols and designs precoding vectors for each user, making precoding-after-compress suitable for distributed precoding.
elements.sent-2411-707: As another option inspired by CoF in the uplink transmission, the CPU can send linear combinations of the users' codewords to the RUs using structured lattice codes.
elements.sent-2411-709: Furthermore, a variant called RQCoF \cite{6522158 can be utilized, where the linear combinations of codewords are quantized at the CPU before being transmitted to the RUs.}
elements.sent-2411-710: In general, when fronthaul capacities are moderate to high, the compression-based strategy consistently outperforms data-sharing, RCoF, and RQCoF strategies, as it achieves low compression distortion.
elements.sent-2411-711: However, in scenarios with small fronthaul capacities, the data-sharing, RCoF, and RQCoF schemes are more practical and offer better performance compared to compression-based strategies, which suffer from high compression distortion \cite{7362826, 6283033 .}
elements.sent-2411-712: To achieve maximum end-to-end performance, it is necessary to optimize both the uplink and downlink schemes.
elements.sent-2411-713: While the exact characterization of the downlink DAS capacity is still an open problem, most research studies have focused on separately or jointly optimizing the schemes using uplink-downlink duality to achieve downlink rates that are greater than or equal to the uplink rates \cite{7541570, 8635883, 9893884, 9687122,miretti2023uldl .}
elements.sent-2411-714: A Comparison of the downlink coding strategies is illustrated in Table \ref{downlink_coding .}
elements.figure-2411-018: \caption{Comparison of the downlink coding strategies} \begin{tabular}{P{0.1\linewidth}P{0.25\linewidth}P{0.25\linewidth}P{0.2\linewidth}} Scheme Processing at CPU Processing at RU References Data Sharing Sharing users' messages with RUs Broadcast the codewords \cite{5997324,6920005,8359129,8732995} Independent Compression Encode, beamform, and compress users' signals Decompress the compressed signals independently \cite{quek_peng_simeone_yu_2017,6588350} Multivariate Compression Jointly compress users' signals Jointly decompress the compressed signals \cite{6588350} CAP Precoding design and compression Decompress the compressed signals \cite{8690797,8902784,8678745} Precoding-After-Compress Simple compression of users' signals Precoding design and Decompression \cite{10.1007/s11277-018-6038-1} RCoF Compute integer linear combinations of the codewords Recover the codewords \cite{6283033} RQCoF Compute integer linear combinations of the codewords, then compress these combinations Decompress the compressed equations and recover the codewords \cite{6522158} \end{tabular} \label{downlink_coding}
elements.sent-2411-715: This section illustrates different uplink and downlink relaying strategies in DAS that aim to optimize network performance while considering the limited fronthaul capacity constraints.
elements.sent-2411-716: The fronthaul links connecting the distributed RUs with the CPU pose a limitation on the amount of traffic exchanged between them.
elements.sent-2411-717: For the uplink transmission from users to the CPU, various relaying schemes have been proposed including DF, AF, CoF, CF, and CCoF.
elements.sent-2411-718: Each strategy has its advantages and disadvantages in terms of performance, computational complexity, and sensitivity to channel conditions.
elements.sent-2411-719: In the downlink transmission from the CPU to users, cooperative relaying schemes are used.
elements.sent-2411-720: Techniques like data-sharing, compression-based strategies, RCoF, and RQCoF have been studied.
elements.sent-2411-721: These techniques leverage the distributed RUs to improve network performance by relaying data from the CPU to users.
elements.sent-2411-722: The optimal relaying strategy is still an active area of research.
elements.sent-2411-723: The optimal relaying strategy for DAS architectures is still an open research question.
elements.sent-2411-724: Various optimization methods and frameworks have been proposed to find appropriate solutions based on the specific requirements and constraints of the network.
elements.sent-2411-725: These methods involve the optimization of coding schemes, power allocation, compression levels, and decoding strategies.
elements.sent-2411-726: Generally, the choice of relaying strategy depends on the specific DAS architecture, fronthaul capacity constraints, and desired network performance.
elements.sent-2411-727: By studying and optimizing these strategies, valuable insights have been gained on how to effectively utilize limited fronthaul capacity in DAS and enhance network performance.
elements.sent-2411-728: In this section, we review some optimization techniques that aim to improve the performance of DAS networks in terms of data rate, energy efficiency, coverage probability, and systems' fairness.
elements.sent-2411-729: Some of these optimization techniques include channel estimation, power control, resource allocation, and downlink/uplink beamforming.
elements.sent-2411-730: Then we investigate the physical layer operations and how these operations should be optimized.
elements.sent-2411-731: In addition, we discuss the integration of DAS networks in emerging technologies such as RISs, optical wireless communications (OWCs), and non-terrestrial networks.
elements.sent-2411-732: One of the main operations that should be implemented at transmitters and receivers is channel estimation.
elements.sent-2411-733: Channel estimation is an important procedure to improve the uplink and downlink transmission performance.
elements.sent-2411-734: Acquiring accurate CSI helps in mitigating interference, reducing the bit error rate, and designing robust beamforming techniques.
elements.sent-2411-735: There are channel estimation schemes that are designed for time division duplex (TDD) systems and others that are designed for frequency division duplex systems.
elements.sent-2411-736: Since the channels in the frequency division duplex system are not reciprocal, it needs a much larger overhead than the TDD scheme \cite{6449246 .}
elements.sent-2411-737: Thus, we focus more on TDD scheme since the frequency division duplex system is not common in DAS networks.
elements.sent-2411-738: In TDD, each coherence block is divided into three different phases: A channel estimation phase, a downlink data transmission phase, and an uplink data transmission phase \cite{R21 .}
elements.sent-2411-739: In the channel estimation phase, the users send uplink pilot signals to the APs, which estimate the channel of each user from these pilots.
elements.sent-2411-740: Then the CSI parameters are transmitted to the cloud for detecting the received signals and beamforming the transmitted signals exploiting the channels reciprocity \cite{R6 .}
elements.sent-2411-741: In particular, the cloud exploits the fact that the uplink channel is the same as the downlink channel within each coherence block.
elements.sent-2411-742: The TDD channel estimation overhead scales linearly with increasing the number of users and is independent of the number of APs \cite{8387197 .}
elements.sent-2411-743: At the user end, the users may rely on the channel hardening when decoding their received signals, or estimate the channel from the received signals to avoid downlink pilot overhead \cite{R6 .}
elements.sent-2411-744: However, in DAS networks, the authors of \cite{8379438, 8799031 showed that CF-mMIMO provides a low degree of channel hardening compared to the co-located massive MIMO systems.}
elements.sent-2411-745: They showed that the downlink channel is better to be estimated either using pilot transmission at the expense of extra overhead or using the received signal.
elements.sent-2411-746: The authors of \cite{8799031 proposed that instead of estimating the individual channel of each AP at the users, the APs can send the same pilots to the users (i.e., the user $i$ will receive the same pilot signals from all the APs).}
elements.sent-2411-747: Therefore, the users can estimate the accumulated channel from all APs.
elements.sent-2411-748: One main issue in DASs with massive MIMO systems is that the channel estimation operations should be selected to allow system scalability.
elements.sent-2411-749: Scaling the system to a larger system can be implemented by proposing techniques that reduce the fronthaul overhead as the number of users increases, proposing simple beamforming and power allocation techniques, and reducing the exchanged data among APs.
elements.sent-2411-750: One way to reduce the exchanged CSI among APs is to propose beamforming approaches based on local CSI estimation\footnote{Local CSI of AP $i$ refers to the channels between the APs $i$ and all users (i.e. each AP does not know the channels between the other APs and users).. .}
elements.sent-2411-751: To accurately estimate the local CSI, orthogonal pilots should be assigned for all users.
elements.sent-2411-752: However, if the number of users is high or the channel coherence interval is small, it is roughly impossible to assign a unique orthogonal pilot to each user.
elements.sent-2411-753: Therefore, pilot reuse or non-orthogonal pilots are used for channel estimation, which causes a phenomenon called pilot contamination.
elements.sent-2411-754: Users that use the same pilot or non-orthogonal pilots experience mutual interference.
elements.sent-2411-755: In particular, pilot contamination leads to increasing the channel estimation error and the correlation of the channel estimates of the users that use non-orthogonal pilots \cite{R20 .}
elements.sent-2411-756: To mitigate the effect of pilot contamination, the pilots should be allocated to the users efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-757: The authors of \cite{6812159 characterized the low-rankness of covariance matrices of the users' channels for improving the channel estimation using pilot decontamination and mitigating the interference using spatial filtering.}
elements.sent-2411-758: The authors of \cite{R3 , \cite{R5 compared the greedy pilot assignment with the random assignment approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-759: In the random pilot assignment approach, each user is assigned a random pilot, but there is a probability that two nearby users are assigned to the same pilot, which leads to high mutual interference.
elements.sent-2411-760: In the greedy approach, the pilots are first distributed to the users randomly, and then the user with the lowest achievable rate updates its pilot sequence in a way to minimize the effect of the pilot contamination.
elements.sent-2411-761: The authors of \cite{8780756 proposed a location-based greedy pilot assignment approach exploiting the location information in assigning pilots rather than using the random assignment as an initial step for the greedy approach.}
elements.sent-2411-762: The authors of \cite{R25, R26 proposed a structured pilot assignment, where each two adjacent users are guaranteed to use different pilot sequences to ensure marginal pilot contamination.}
elements.sent-2411-763: In \cite{8914726 , the authors proposed a pilot assignment approach based on a tabu-search method to reduce the high pilot contamination effect.}
elements.sent-2411-764: Under the user-centric user association approach, the authors of \cite{7080877 proposed to assign the pilots to the users such that the users that are served by the same BS in their cluster must be assigned with orthogonal pilots.}
elements.sent-2411-765: The pilots were allocated based on a graph coloring algorithm such as the Dsatur algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-766: To minimize the channel estimation overhead in ultra-dense C-RANs, the authors of \cite{8247283 proposed to estimate only the intra-cluster CSIs and depend only on the large-scale channel information of the inter-cluster CSI.}
elements.sent-2411-767: The authors also applied the Dsatur algorithm to find the minimum number of required pilots that can be assigned to all the users in the system.
elements.sent-2411-768: Another way to reduce the channel estimation overhead is to consider non-orthogonal pilot allocation.
elements.sent-2411-769: The study in \cite{9154310 formulated a biconvex optimization problem to jointly estimate the channels and detect the data symbols.}
elements.sent-2411-770: The problem was solved using the forward-backward splitting approach and their proposed scheme reduced the channel estimation overhead by more than 2 times compared to the orthogonal pilot sequence-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-771: In \cite{9201112 , the authors proposed to estimate the channels via a superimposed technique, where data symbols and pilot signals are transmitted simultaneously within the coherence intervals and both are correlated to a certain level.}
elements.sent-2411-772: The authors showed that the proposed superimposed pilot transmission outperforms the traditional pilot transmission in terms of normalized mean-squared error.
elements.sent-2411-773: To decrease the computational complexity of the CSI acquisition, deep-learning-based approaches have been introduced to estimate and/or predict the required CSI, and they proved an observable improvement compared to traditional approaches.
elements.sent-2411-774: The authors of \cite{8815888 employed denoising convolutional neural networks to provide fast and accurate channel estimates in cell-free mmWave massive MIMO systems.}
elements.sent-2411-775: To exploit the temporal correlation over the coherence intervals, the authors of \cite{obeed2022alternating developed an alternating channel estimation and prediction scheme in order to reduce the channel estimation overhead and improve the system throughput.}
elements.sent-2411-776: The considered scheme in \cite{obeed2022alternating proposed that each user sends a pilot signal in a given number of coherence intervals, where minimum mean-squared error (MMSE) was used to estimate the channels of this coherence interval and a deep neural network was used to predict the channels of the remaining coherence intervals.}
elements.sent-2411-777: In this way, the overhead is reduced by at least $50\%$, which leads to improving the system throughput.
elements.subsection-2411-013: Transmit Precoding and Receive Combining Optimization
elements.sent-2411-778: In DASs, precoding design at transmitters and combining design at receivers are required to maximize achievable rates, energy efficiency, minimum rates, or secrecy rates.
elements.sent-2411-779: For downlink transmission, the distributed APs first estimate the users' channels, then use them to simultaneously beamform the signals to the users using the same time and frequency resources.
elements.sent-2411-780: Whereas, in uplink transmission, all the users transmit their signals to the distributed APs, which in turn use combining techniques to achieve the desired QoS (e.g., sum-rate).
elements.sent-2411-781: The beamforming and combining techniques can be classified in the literature into three techniques: 1) fully centralized, 2) fully distributed, and 3) partially distributed.
elements.sent-2411-782: In fully centralized techniques, the detection and beamforming techniques are implemented at the CPU, which means that the channel estimates of all users must be collected at the CPU/cloud.
elements.sent-2411-783: The main advantage of centralized implementation is that the best beamforming/combining techniques can be employed due to the availability of global CSI at a central entity.
elements.sent-2411-784: For uplink transmission, several papers considered centralized detection schemes, e.g., \cite{R45, 7869024, 7465790 .}
elements.sent-2411-785: The authors of \cite{R45 proposed to use the MMSE-combining as a scheme that utilizes the global CSI to improve detection performance\footnote{The MMSE combiner is a vector that is designed to minimize the mean squared error between the actual and the estimated data signals given the estimated channels \cite{R21 .. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-786: They also showed that using the optimal MMSE processing in a centralized implementation improves the spectral efficiency and reduces the fronthaul overhead.
elements.sent-2411-787: The authors of \cite{7869024 proposed to use MMSE-combining to maximize the received signal-to-interference and noise ratio of each user.}
elements.sent-2411-788: The authors in \cite{7465790 proposed to use a weighted-MMSE-combining with successive convex approximation to design the transmit beamformers and the quantization noise covariance matrices to maximize the weighted sum-rate of multi-antennas users at multi-antennas APs in a C-RAN system.}
elements.sent-2411-789: Note that these centralized precoding or detection schemes require global CSI availability at the CPU, which limits their scalability, especially in CF-mMIMO systems.
elements.sent-2411-790: For scalable systems, low-complexity precoding approaches that can be implemented by knowing local CSI only are more practical \cite{R20 .}
elements.sent-2411-791: To reduce the MMSE combiner complexity, the authors of \cite{7869024 , \cite{R20 proposed the partial-MMSE combiner that considers only the channel estimates of the users that are served partially by the same APs.}}
elements.sent-2411-792: Partial-MMSE reduces the complexity and number of the required channel estimates to calculate the beamforming vector at the expense of a slight increase in the received interference.
elements.sent-2411-793: To guarantee scalable cell-free systems, the authors proposed a simpler precoding approach, called local-PMMSE, where each AP only uses the channel estimates of the connected users to design the beamformer for each user.
elements.sent-2411-794: In local-PMMSE, there is no need to forward the channel estimates to the CPU since each AP can design the beamformer (combiner) locally, and this alleviates a burden on the fronthaul links \cite{R20 .}
elements.sent-2411-795: Based on a given serving APs for each user, the authors of \cite{R20 showed that the partial-MMSE approach outperforms the local-PMMSE, which in turn outperforms the conjugate beamforming in terms of achievable rate performance.}
elements.sent-2411-796: Using only the large-scale fading information to design beamformers and combiners is desirable in real-life systems due to its low computational complexity and low fronthaul signaling \cite{ 7869024, 9739189 .}
elements.sent-2411-797: Based on large-scale fading decoding, the authors of \cite{9739189 proposed to split the uplink decoding process between the CPU and the APs to trade-off between the fronthaul signaling and the spectral efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-798: For downlink transmission, the authors of \cite{1683918 showed that the optimal precoding approach in multiuser MIMO systems is dirty paper coding.}
elements.sent-2411-799: However, due to its high complexity, dirty paper coding is not a practical solution in CF-mMIMO systems.
elements.sent-2411-800: Hence, linear centralized precoding approaches are proposed and investigated in CF-mMIMO systems \cite{R12,R34 .}
elements.sent-2411-801: The authors of \cite{R34 used maximum ratio transmission and zero-forcing precoding approaches to mitigate the interference at the users.}
elements.sent-2411-802: They showed that the zero-forcing beamforming approach significantly outperforms the maximum ratio transmission approach.
elements.sent-2411-803: In the zero-forcing scheme, the precoding vector of each user is designed to be orthogonal to the channels of all other users.
elements.sent-2411-804: Different from the distributed precoding, the authors of \cite{R12 showed that the average rate of users increases unboundedly as the numbers of antennas and users increase if a centralized precoding scheme is used (e.g., zero-forcing).}
elements.sent-2411-805: A more advanced precoding scheme was proposed in \cite{R20 , which is the MMSE precoding.}
elements.sent-2411-806: The MMSE approach optimizes the combining weights at each AP to minimize the mean square error between the combined signal and the desired signal.
elements.sent-2411-807: It takes into account the channel conditions, interference, and noise statistics to improve the overall received signal quality.
elements.sent-2411-808: In \cite{8579566 , the authors designed beamforming vectors to minimize the total consumed power subject to an achievable rate constraint when the CSI of the users is imperfectly known.}
elements.sent-2411-809: The authors used an iterative algorithm and Lagrange dual decomposition method to find an optimal solution for the downlink beamforming vectors.
elements.sent-2411-810: In \cite{8660693 , the authors jointly optimized the downlink transmission for hybrid analog-digital antenna arrays in C-RAN systems.}
elements.sent-2411-811: In particular, the authors used a weighted-MMSE approach and block coordinate descent to jointly design the RF beamforming, the digital beamforming, and the fronthaul compression to maximize the weighted sum-rate.
elements.sent-2411-812: Due to its simplicity and distributed nature, the authors of \cite{R3 , \cite{R5 , \cite{R6 proposed to use the conjugate beamforming approach.}}}
elements.sent-2411-813: The authors of \cite{R34 compared the performance of the conjugate beamforming approach with a low-complexity zero-forcing beamforming approach.}
elements.sent-2411-814: At the expense of fronthaul load, authors of \cite{R34 showed that the proposed low-complexity zero-forcing approach outperforms the conjugate beamforming significantly.}
elements.sent-2411-815: The authors of \cite{8599043 proposed a modified conjugate beamforming scheme that eliminates self-interference completely, while preserving the simplicity of the conjugate beamforming approach, at the expense of exchanging limited channel information among APs.}
elements.sent-2411-816: To avoid the need for information exchange among APs, the authors of \cite{9340389 proposed an enhanced conjugate beamforming technique, where the beamforming vector is normalized with respect to the channel estimate.}
elements.sent-2411-817: The aim of using this technique is to boost the channel hardening at the users which improves the downlink spectral efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-818: The authors of \cite{9108198 designed an optimal beamformer using a bisection algorithm to maximize the signal-to-interference and noise ratio among all users.}
elements.sent-2411-819: The authors of \cite{atzeni2020distributed proposed a distributed cooperative precoding that overcomes the need for fronthaul transmission of CSI exchange.}
elements.sent-2411-820: They achieve that by engaging in an over-the-air signaling approach.
elements.sent-2411-821: This approach guarantees that the total signaling amount does not scale up with the number of users or the APs.
elements.sent-2411-822: Four coordination levels among APs were investigated in \cite{R45 to provide a thorough analysis of uplink CF-mMIMO systems.}
elements.sent-2411-823: The authors concluded that CF-mMIMO can significantly outperform small cells and cellular massive MIMO by using local or global MMSE combining.
elements.sent-2411-824: Although spreading antennas over areas reduces latency and improves data rates, coverage, and fairness, it presents several challenges such as deployment cost, power consumption, fronthaul issues, signal processing complexity, etc. To mitigate the effect of these challenges, optimizing DAS operations is required.
elements.sent-2411-825: Scaling DAS over wide areas increases the number of served users.
elements.sent-2411-826: This increases the channel estimation overhead or makes the pilot contamination more severe.
elements.sent-2411-827: Therefore, intelligent channel estimation methods are required to manage pilot contamination and make DAS more scalable.
elements.sent-2411-828: Most of the recent works argued that deep learning-based channel estimation methods are less complex and provide more accurate estimates compared to traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-829: This is due to the fact that deep learning-based methods fuse spectral, temporal, and spatial information to provide a deeper understanding of the channel change over spectrum, time, and space.
elements.sent-2411-830: However, due to the explosive increase in the number of users and the 6G promises to provide a decent QoS for very high-mobility users, the existing approaches to reduce pilot contamination or channel estimation overhead may not be satisfactory to meet the expected requirements.
elements.sent-2411-831: Since DASs promise to spread a massive number of distributed APs, interference must be managed efficiently to avoid degrading the rates of the end users.
elements.sent-2411-832: The common way to manage the interference is to propose robust transmit precoding and receive combining approaches.
elements.sent-2411-833: However, this requires the DAS to be more centralized so the interference among APs can be mitigated.
elements.sent-2411-834: For instance, adopting the conjugate beamforming approach for transmission would produce significant interference at the users, while the zero-forcing or the MMSE precoding approaches (which require CSI sharing among APs) would significantly reduce the interference.
elements.sent-2411-835: The existing literature indicated that the user-centric design is the solution to balance between managing the interference and making DASs more scalable.
elements.sent-2411-836: In such a design, the range of the serving APs for each user must be addressed carefully to manage this trade-off efficiently.
elements.section-2411-007: Integration of DAS with Other Communication Systems
elements.sent-2411-837: In this subsection, we review existing works that considered the DAS in different communication systems.
elements.sent-2411-838: We focus on the integration of DAS with RIS-assisted communication systems, OWC systems, and satellite networks.
elements.sent-2411-839: An RIS is a two-dimensional surface consisting of several reflecting elements whose phases and amplitudes can be digitally-controlled to reflect the incident signals in desirable directions (i.e., change the phase of the incident signals) \cite{alouiniR26 .}
elements.sent-2411-840: The main advantages of these surfaces are: i) ease of deployment, ii) low-cost, iii) passive nature, and iv) low-energy consumption \cite{9756313 .}
elements.sent-2411-841: In contrast, each antenna in DASs must be associated with an RF chain and digital circuits to implement signal processing operations leading to a higher cost and energy consumption compared to a reflecting element.
elements.sent-2411-842: Despite this advantage of RIS elements compared to antennas, their contribution to the system performance is low compared to an antenna due to the double fading effect.
elements.sent-2411-843: Nonetheless, the deployment of multiple RISs can support DASs through reducing the number of APs, thus improving the energy efficiency of the system.
elements.sent-2411-844: Several works studied the roles that RISs can play in DASs \cite{10431714, 9473521, 9298890, 9352948, 9448858, 9665300, 9911685 .}
elements.sent-2411-845: The authors of \cite{10431714, 9473521 showed that deploying RISs in cell-free networks helps in extending the system coverage and maximizing the minimum users achievable rates.}
elements.sent-2411-846: The authors showed that the RIS provides nearly a 2-fold increase in the minimum rate and a 1.5-fold increase in the per-user rate.
elements.sent-2411-847: The authors of \cite{9298890 formulated and solved the problem of maximizing the weighted sum-rate via optimizing the beamforming matrices at the APs and the RISs of an RIS-aided cell-free system.}
elements.sent-2411-848: The study in \cite{9352948 proposed to aid the CF-mMIMO systems with multiple RISs to improve the energy efficiency of the system.}
elements.sent-2411-849: The problem in \cite{9352948 was formulated to maximize energy efficiency by optimizing the beamforming matrices at the BSs and RISs.}
elements.sent-2411-850: It is concluded that the energy efficiency increases with increasing the number of RISs and their sizes to some point where it starts to decrease \cite{9298890,9352948 .}
elements.sent-2411-851: This decrease is due to the increase of the hardware static power consumption of each RIS with the number of elements in each RIS.
elements.sent-2411-852: The authors of \cite{9448858 showed that the energy efficiency of the worst user in a wideband cell-free system can be improved by employing multiple RISs.}
elements.sent-2411-853: One of the practical challenges in RIS and DASs is the channel estimation overhead and the spatial correlation among RIS's reflecting elements.
elements.sent-2411-854: Hence, the authors of \cite{9665300 investigated the effect of the channels' spatial correlation and proposed a simple channel estimation scheme that takes into account the overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-855: The authors also optimized the phase shifts to minimize the channel estimation error and derived a closed form for the ergodic downlink and uplink net throughputs.
elements.sent-2411-856: They concluded that the contribution of RIS to cell-free systems is high when the AP-user links are weak with a high probability.
elements.sent-2411-857: The authors of \cite{10640072 proposed a reflection pattern modulation-aided RIS-assisted CF-mMIMO system for energy-efficient uplink transmission.}
elements.sent-2411-858: They introduced a system that optimizes the energy versus spectral efficiency trade-off by activating only part of the RIS blocks.
elements.sent-2411-859: RISs can also be used to improve the secrecy rate of different communication systems \cite{8723525, 9206080 .}
elements.sent-2411-860: The authors of \cite{9911685 investigated the role of RISs in improving the secrecy rate of a cell-free system.}
elements.sent-2411-861: The authors utilized semi-definite relaxation and successive convex approximation to jointly optimize the beamforming of the APs and RISs in order to maximize the weighted secrecy rate of the system.
elements.sent-2411-862: Recently, RIS was integrated with terrestrial distributed networks to improve different metrics, such as network connectivity \cite{alabiad2023effectivenessreconfigurableintelligentsurfaces, 10437661 and energy efficiency \cite{10437618 .}}
elements.sent-2411-863: RF communication and OWC use different spectrums to transmit messages.
elements.sent-2411-864: Each has its own advantages and disadvantages.
elements.sent-2411-865: On the one hand, OWC provides high data rates, but has some limitations such as the blockage effects and the significant channel attenuation with distance.
elements.sent-2411-866: On the other hand, RF can provide ubiquitous connectivity but with lower data rates.
elements.sent-2411-867: Thus OWC and RF communication systems can complement each other by mitigating each other's drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-868: Integrating these systems can lead to reducing the received interference (RF spectrum doesn't interfere with the optical spectrum), improving the achievable data rates, and improving the system’s fairness.
elements.sent-2411-869: The authors of \cite{8113550, 8779676 investigated a visible-light communication (VLC) CF-mMIMO system and studied the problem of user association.}
elements.sent-2411-870: The authors in \cite{8574984 studied a VLC DAS, where the APs and the users are assigned to each other based on a user-centric approach aiming at maximizing energy efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-871: The authors of \cite{liu2022joint suggested employing the cell-free concept to reduce the potentially excessive handover in VLC systems which is caused by their small cell size.}
elements.sent-2411-872: The authors proposed a joint AP grouping and user clustering to manage the interference.
elements.sent-2411-873: The authors of \cite{almehdhar2022hybrid and \cite{almehdhar2022user introduced the cell-free concept into indoor hybrid VLC/RF networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-874: However, the studies in \cite{almehdhar2022hybrid, almehdhar2022user only focused on associating the users to OWC and RF networks without considering the problems of power allocation, beamforming design, resource allocation, channel estimation, and clustering formation.}
elements.sent-2411-875: One limitation of terrestrial networks is their limited ability to provide ubiquitous connectivity to rural areas.
elements.sent-2411-876: Although terrestrial DAS have been proposed to improve wireless coverage, their deployment cost to cover wide areas is prohibitive.
elements.sent-2411-877: In addition, terrestrial networks in general fail to provide services when natural disasters happen.
elements.sent-2411-878: Therefore, researchers increased their focus on studying non-terrestrial networks and how they integrate with terrestrial networks.
elements.sent-2411-879: In the integrated terrestrial and non-terrestrial networks, a 3-dimensional (3D) network architecture (consisting of terrestrial APs and aerial APs) is dedicated to enhancing the coverage and providing connectivity to unconnected areas \cite{giordani2020non .}
elements.sent-2411-880: In particular, satellites, high-altitude platform systems, and aerial unmanned vehicles (UAVs) are envisioned to integrate with terrestrial networks to meet the 6G stringent requirement of ubiquitous connectivity \cite{giordani2020non .}
elements.sent-2411-881: The authors of \cite{riera2022scalable proposed to use low-earth orbit satellites to support terrestrial DAS in order to maximize the minimum per-user rate and the sum-rate of the integrated system.}
elements.sent-2411-882: The approach in \cite{riera2022scalable transfers the users that limit the terrestrial DAS performance to be served by the satellite segment.}
elements.sent-2411-883: Due to the long distance between the satellites and the handheld devices, the link attenuation is high and direct-to-satellite (i.e., direct from a handheld device to a satellite without a ground station assistance) communication is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-884: Therefore, the authors of \cite{abdelsadek2023broadband proposed employing the DAS concept in satellite networks to improve the data rates at handheld devices.}
elements.sent-2411-885: This can be enabled by the expected ultra-dense low-earth orbit satellite deployment and the interconnection between the satellites through optical links.
elements.sent-2411-886: The authors of \cite{abdelsadek2021future studied the problems of optimizing pilot assignment, duplexing mode, power allocation, beamforming, and handover management in satellite DAS.}
elements.sent-2411-887: The authors of \cite{10622597 investigated a user-centric multi-satellite communication system aimed at maximizing the weighted sum rates of users through joint beamforming.}
elements.sent-2411-888: A centralized algorithm was proposed, assuming perfect CSI, alongside a low-complexity distributed algorithm using multi-agent deep reinforcement learning for practical implementation.
elements.sent-2411-889: Aiming at maximizing the uplink spectral efficiency for the ground users, the authors of \cite{10634184 proposed a new CF-mMIMO architecture combining low Earth orbit satellites with UAVs as flying APs.}
elements.sent-2411-890: The authors derived expressions for direct and cascaded channels, proposed local combining strategies, and introduced a dynamic clustering framework to reduce interference and enhance user service.
elements.sent-2411-891: Although a few papers studied the integration of satellite and terrestrial networks using the DAS concept, many problems are untapped and need to be tackled in future works.
elements.sent-2411-892: Examples of these problems are studying the Doppler effect, latency, synchronization, user association, backhaul issues, and CPU selection.
elements.sent-2411-893: In Section \ref{Sec:Integration , we discuss these issues in detail.}
elements.sent-2411-894: In the next section, we discuss cross-layer optimization techniques that consider both the physical layer and network layer viewpoints of DASs.
elements.section-2411-008: Cross-Layer Optimization for Resource Allocation in DASs
elements.sent-2411-895: Resource allocation via cross-layer optimization is crucial to achieve high performance in DASs, e.g., C-RANs, F-RANs, and D2D-aided F-RANs.
elements.sent-2411-896: Cross-layer optimization (i.e., physical and network layers joint optimization) can target different QoS metrics, including throughput/sum-rate maximization, cloud offloading maximization, and delay minimization under a QoS constraint.
elements.sent-2411-897: This is usually presented through maximizing a wide-network utility or minimizing a cost function that considers user scheduling using NC and power allocation with some constraints.
elements.sent-2411-898: The cross-layer performance of the aforementioned systems is constrained by the scarcity of radio resources and the limited capacity of fronthaul links.
elements.sent-2411-899: To efficiently utilize the available RBs in such systems, traditional schemes consider assigning a single user to each RB on each RRH, thus transmitting only one content from that RB/RRH.
elements.sent-2411-900: However, this single user assignment becomes highly challenging with the tremendous increase in the number of users nowadays.
elements.sent-2411-901: Consequently, NC \cite{NC1 is employed to mix users' data (packets) and simultaneously allocate different users to the same RB.}
elements.sent-2411-902: To assess the merits of NC, consider a network composed of one transmitter and two users each requesting one packet and already has obtained the other packet.
elements.sent-2411-903: Without NC (i.e., uncoded), the transmitter needs two transmissions to complete the reception of all packets at both users.
elements.sent-2411-904: However, NC reduces the number to a single transmission by encoding both packets.
elements.sent-2411-905: The requested and received packets of users are known in the literature as side information \cite{NC1 .}
elements.sent-2411-906: By combining this side information using NC, multiple users can be served simultaneously on the same RB/RRH.
elements.sent-2411-907: Thus, significant improvements can be achieved in transmission efficiency, content delivery, throughput, and delay in DAS.
elements.sent-2411-908: This section discusses the optimization of DASs from a cross-layer perspective using NC.
elements.sent-2411-909: Since 2000, NC, initiated by Ahlswede et al. \cite{NC1 , has drawn researchers' attention as a method for improving content delivery and achieving maximum information flow in different communication networks, e.g., \cite{NC2, NC3, NC4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-910: NC maximizes information flow by combining users' data at the source and intermediate nodes in a network \cite{NC1 (in the network layer) and sending the combination instead of sending the raw data as in the simple RF (i.e., uncoded).}
elements.sent-2411-911: For a system of many users and packets, the key potential of NC is to ensure partial or full reception of the packets at multiple users, while reducing data traffic by sending combined packets.
elements.sent-2411-912: This enables maximizing the throughput and minimizing the communication delay.
elements.sent-2411-913: Based on the application, two NC design methods are distinguished in the literature, random linear NC (RLNC) and instantly decodable NC (IDNC).
elements.sent-2411-914: In RLNC, at each transmission, the sender broadcasts a coded packet obtained by combining all source packets using random and independent coefficients generated from a given Galois Field \cite{RLNC_1,RLNC_2, RLNC_3, RLNC_4, 4557282 .}
elements.sent-2411-915: RLNC has drawn much interest of the research community due to its (i) ability to recover packets without feedback and (ii) optimality in reducing the number of packet transmissions in broadcast scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-916: It was initially used in point-to-multipoint models and then expanded in different systems, including multipoint-to-multipoint \cite{RLNC_1 and D2D communications \cite{9036050 .}}
elements.sent-2411-917: However, it is only suitable for applications that tolerate high delay as it does not support progressive and instant packet decoding.
elements.sent-2411-918: Moreover, it is inefficient in multicast scenarios that different groups of users are interested in different subsets of packets.
elements.sent-2411-919: From a complexity point of view, the computational complexity of decoding in RLNC scales cubically with the number of packets \cite{RLNC_2 , which is prohibitive for battery-powered devices.}
elements.sent-2411-920: On the other hand, IDNC is a good candidate to overcome the aforementioned issues of RLNC, especially for applications that require real-time optimization, such as video streaming.
elements.sent-2411-921: It relies on progressive and instantaneous decoding, which means that the received coded packets are decoded instantly, and the retrieved packets are used immediately at the application layer.
elements.sent-2411-922: IDNC exploits users' side information in the network to enhance performance.
elements.sent-2411-923: In IDNC, the instantaneous decoding of the requested packets is achieved by simple encoding and decoding operations.
elements.sent-2411-924: The transmitter combines packets using binary XOR, which can efficiently be implemented on a device.
elements.sent-2411-925: At the user side, packets are decoded at their reception instant using binary XOR.
elements.sent-2411-926: Due to its simple encoding/decoding operation and instant decodability, IDNC has shown excellent abilities to substantially improve transmission efficiency, packet recovery, throughput and delay over broadcast erasure channels \cite{NC1, NC2, NC3, NC4, 7845689, 8794557, 9512273, 9180294, 8267199, 8444481, 6882208, 1176612, 4557282, 1705002, 4675714, 6874566, 9982443, 10229924 .}
elements.sent-2411-927: Such simple features led to tremendous progress in the analysis and the development of simple online algorithm design for IDNC that can be represented as a graph model, known as IDNC graph.
elements.sent-2411-928: To represent all packet combinations that are instantly decodable by a subset or all users, the authors in \cite{5683677 originally proposed the IDNC graph.}
elements.sent-2411-929: Fig. \ref{fig2_NC illustrates the construction of the IDNC graph in a simple C-RAN model with one RRH that is given in Fig. \ref{fig1_NC .}}
elements.sent-2411-930: The arbitrary users' side information is shown on the right side of Fig. \ref{fig1_NC .}
elements.sent-2411-931: Based on users' side information, the corresponding IDNC graph is constructed by first generating a vertex for each user and each packet, e.g., packet $p_3$ that is requested by user $1$ is represented by a vertex $(1, 3)$.
elements.sent-2411-932: The fundamental idea of constructing the IDNC graph is to find a feasible packet combination with this feature: two packets are wanted by two users are combinable if these two users can both benefit from the combined packet, making it instantly decodable for both users.
elements.sent-2411-933: This situation happens if and only if the two users are requesting the same packet or if the packet wanted by each user is already received at the other user.
elements.sent-2411-934: The goal of IDNC is to satisfy many users with one combined packet.
elements.sent-2411-935: For example, the RRH transmits the combination $p_1\oplus p_3$ to users $1, 2, 3$, $4$.
elements.sent-2411-936: In general, finding the optimal packet combination on such graphs that provides the highest objective function to a given network's metric requires an exhaustive search over all possible packet combinations, which is clearly non-feasible for large network sizes \cite{7845689 .}
elements.sent-2411-937: Without loss of generality, solving the IDNC-related problems (e.g., completion time and decoding delay that are extensively investigated in \cite{6882208, 8444481, 9180294, 8794557, 9512273, 9391701 ) is equivalent to solving the maximum clique problem \cite{5683677 , which is nondeterministic polynomial-hard \cite{10.5555/574848 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-938: A detailed analysis of the developed algorithms, complexity, and applications of maximum clique problems can be found in \cite{9044331 .}

The side information of users is also shown, where each user has received some packets and wants some other packets. For example, user $1$ already received packets $p_1$, $p_2$, $p_4$ and requests $p_3$.} \label{fig1_NC} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-020: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{fig3_NC} \caption{IDNC graph representing the coding possibilities for the system depicted in Fig. \ref{fig1_NC}. Each vertex represents a requested packet by one user, i.e., vertex $(2, 1)$ represents the indices of user $2$ and packet $1$. Each clique in the IDNC graph represents a feasible IDNC combination. For example, one possible clique is: $\{(1, 3), (2, 1), (3, 1), (4, 3)\}$ that corresponds to transmitting the combination $1\oplus 3$ to the targeted users $1$, $2$, $3$, and $4$.} \label{fig2_NC} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-939: IDNC optimizes different performance metrics of DAS (e.g., completion time and decoding delay) by combining users' data in the network layer and sending the combination to multiple users.
elements.sent-2411-940: However, it abstracts the physical layer conditions to packet erasures \cite{8794557, 9512273, 9391701 .}
elements.sent-2411-941: Thus, IDNC ignores the channel heterogeneous capacities of different users in the selection of a combined packet.
elements.sent-2411-942: Instead, the transmitter of an IDNC combination selects the minimum achievable capacity of all the associated users in the combination.
elements.sent-2411-943: This results in serving a large number of users but unfortunately leads to physical layer throughput degradation, which degrades the physical-layer performance of DAS.
elements.sent-2411-944: Studying the trade-off between the performances of the network layer and the physical layer from a content delivery perspective in different system models of DAS has been a topic of research interest for years \cite{7511238, 7864466, 6662474, 5072351, 5424026,5462127, 4567144, 6692154, 9519548 .}
elements.sent-2411-945: On the one hand, transmit a combined IDNC packet with a high rate will take a shorter time but will be only successfully received by users with high channel capacities.
elements.sent-2411-946: On the other hand, transmit a combined IDNC packet with a lower rate will be received by more users but will take a longer delivery time.
elements.sent-2411-947: To improve the physical layer performance and ensure the QoS of users of DAS, the authors of \cite{7511238, 7864466, 6662474, 5072351, 5424026,5462127, 4567144, 6692154, 9982443 included the channel capacity as another factor in the selection of IDNC packets.}
elements.sent-2411-948: As a result, the IDNC packet selection depends on the side information of the users and their channel capacities.
elements.sent-2411-949: This improved version of IDNC is called rate-aware IDNC (RA-IDNC), which was first studied in \cite{RAIDNC1, 7511238, 6662474 and applied in C-RAN \cite{RAIDNC5 and point-to-multipoint system \cite{6692154 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-950: RA-IDNC has the potential to serve a significant set of users with relatively good transmission rates.
elements.sent-2411-951: Due to this feature, RA-IDNC has been considered in a number of works, e.g., \cite{RAIDNC1, 5072351,5462127, 4567144, 6692154, RAIDNC2, RAIDNC3, 5424026, RAIDNC4 to enhance DAS.}
elements.sent-2411-952: For example, the authors of \cite{RAIDNC2 , \cite{RAIDNC5 proposed RA-IDNC scheme to minimize the completion time in C-RAN systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-953: However, the authors of \cite{RAIDNC5 assumed that each RRH maintains a fixed power and that the transmission rate of all RRHs is determined by the weakest RRH, i.e., the RRH that supports the lowest transmission rate.}
elements.sent-2411-954: Since this reduces the QoS for some users, the authors of \cite{CLNC1 introduced CLNC to optimize the employed rates in RA-IDNC using power control for each RRH in a C-RAN.}
elements.sent-2411-955: In CLNC, combining users' requests depends on NC, users’ rates, and the transmit power of each RRH, which can be jointly optimized to improve the sum-rate \cite{CLNC1, CLNC2, CLNC5a in C-RANs, cloud offloading \cite{CLNC3, CLNC4, CLNC5 in F-RANs, and delay \cite{CLNC6, 9791405 in a F-RAN aided D2D system.}}}
elements.sent-2411-956: The key potential of CLNC is that it balances between cross-layer throughput and network delay with negligible decoding complexity, which ensures a better QoS for real-time streaming applications in DAS.
elements.sent-2411-957: In such applications, users need to stream data from RRHs in DAS (e.g., C-RAN, F-RAN) with the minimum possible delay.
elements.sent-2411-958: Consider that a popular video representing a frame of packets is requested by a set of users located in a playground, where many users want to stream this popular video.
elements.sent-2411-959: At any given time, assume that users have already received some packets and missed some other packets due to channel impairments.
elements.sent-2411-960: To stream that video without any interruption, users should receive their missed packets with minimum delay.
elements.sent-2411-961: One way is to pre-load that frame which can be done at low rates or at off-peak times, both of which are inefficient.
elements.sent-2411-962: CLNC performs a joint optimization of NC and transmit rate and power for efficient and fast file delivery, which meets the delay requirements and streaming quality of DAS.
elements.sent-2411-963: This makes CLNC a promising technique for significantly improving users' QoS, thanks to the following properties.
elements.sent-2411-964: The immediate decodability of the received packets makes them useful at the reception time to meet the real-time streaming application requirements.
elements.sent-2411-965: Encoding at the transmitter is performed using a simple binary XOR operation.
elements.sent-2411-966: Likewise, decoding at the users is performed using a simple binary XOR operation, which overcomes the high computational complexity of RLNC.
elements.sent-2411-967: Since CLNC depends on instant decodability, any received packets that are non-useful for the users are discarded.
elements.sent-2411-968: Thus, CLNC overcomes the need for buffers to store these non-useful packets, which enables the design of cost and energy-efficient devices.
elements.sent-2411-969: CLNC balances between the physical layer and network layer system's performance by adapting a transmission rate and power allocation that supports the smallest channel capacity of all network-coded scheduled users.
elements.sent-2411-970: Table~\ref{table_1NC summarizes the performance of RLNC, IDNC, and CLNC according to various criteria.}

elements.sent-2411-971: Cross-layer optimization of user scheduling and power allocation in DAS can be solved jointly or iteratively, see for example \cite{6588350, 8027131, 7342981, 6811617 .}
elements.sent-2411-972: While the joint solution is expensive in terms of computational complexity, the classical iterative one is sub-optimal.
elements.sent-2411-973: Besides the joint and classic techniques to solve cross-layer optimization problems, graph theory methods have been widely considered in the recent literature of C-RANs, F-RANs, D2D, and F-RAN-aided D2D, e.g., see \cite{RAIDNC1, RAIDNC2, RAIDNC3, RAIDNC4, RAIDNC5, CLNC1, CLNC2,CLNC3,CLNC4,CLNC6, CLNC5a, 9791405 .}
elements.sent-2411-974: Graph theory techniques establish a framework with an exact graph representation of scheduling decisions and objective transformation, and numerous theorems that facilitates the analysis and optimization of a problem.
elements.sent-2411-975: The framework also offers combinatorial techniques that allow the quantitative solving of a problem.
elements.sent-2411-976: In general, researchers utilize graph theory to solve a typical cross-layer optimization problem in DAS, and the solution relies on designing a graph, where its vertices represent the possible scheduling and power levels.
elements.sent-2411-977: The vertices of the graph are connected via edges that satisfy problem's constraints while vertices' weights reflect the objective function.
elements.sent-2411-978: In addition to its application in DAS, graph theory has garnered significant interest in various settings, such as aerial-terrestrial integrated networks for federated learning \cite{10550002, 9844152, 9932020 , RISs \cite{10437661 , and D2D communications \cite{10061474, 10199618 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-979: Unlike the previous works that used either IDNC graph or RA-IDNC graph, the CLNC graph also considers transmission rate and power allocation for multiplexing users.
elements.sent-2411-980: The CLNC graph is introduced in \cite{CLNC1 which takes user multiplexing using NC, rate adaptation, and power allocation into consideration.}
elements.sent-2411-981: An instantly decodable combination that satisfies the CLNC conditions is used to retrieve a new wanted packet by a user if and only if.
elements.sent-2411-982: The user can properly receive the combination with a rate that is below the capacity of the channel between a user and an RRH.
elements.sent-2411-983: The user can re-XOR the combination with its previously received packets to retrieve a new packet.
elements.sent-2411-984: The main concept of CLNC for cross-layer optimization can be illustrated in the following example.
elements.sent-2411-985: Fig. \ref{fig3_NC considers a simple C-RAN model that consists of $2$ RRHs, $3$ users, and $3$ packets.}
elements.sent-2411-986: In order to maximize cross-layer throughput for this example, many possible solutions can be found as summarized in Table~\ref{table_2NC .}
elements.sent-2411-987: For example, the RRH $1$ XORes packets $2$ and $3$ into $2\oplus 3$ and which it transmits with a rate of $2$ bits/s to the set users $2$ and $3$.
elements.sent-2411-988: RRH $2$ transmits packet $1$ with a rate of $2$ bits/s to user $1$.
elements.sent-2411-989: Given this, we have the following decoding process at the users.
elements.sent-2411-990: User $2$ gets packet $2$ by XORing $2\oplus 3$ with packet $3$ and user $3$ gets packet $3$ by XORing packet $2\oplus 3$ with packet $2$.
elements.sent-2411-991: Therefore, the achievable overall throughput in this scenario is $6$ bits/s as each served user will simultaneously receive $2$ bits/s.
elements.sent-2411-992: Table~\ref{table_2NC highlights that the uncoded scheme can only serve one user from each RRH with the maximum transmission rate and IDNC can schedule all the users in the network with the lowest transmission rate of all the scheduled users.}
elements.sent-2411-993: CLNC strikes a balance between these two, thus it is more efficient in maximizing the cross-layer throughput and minimizing the completion time.
elements.sent-2411-994: Table~\ref{table_2NC also depicts the average sum throughput maximization for the considered C-RAN and highlights that the CLNC significantly outperforms the uncoded and IDNC schemes.}
